guidance.q3: How can I support my child's learning at home?

officials.rankLine: Rank {rank} of {n} blocks · best block {best} ({bestScore}) · district {district}
officials.varianceIntro: Share of score variation at each level. Most of the gap is between schools and between children in the same class — not between blocks.
officials.leverageIntro: Schools where focused support recovers the most points for the block average. Framed for targeting help — not a ranking.
officials.halfDeficit: {n} of {total} schools account for half the gap to the block mean.
officials.whatifTitle: What lifting the weakest schools would do
officials.whatifLine: Bringing the weakest {n} schools up to the block average moves the block score by +{delta} (to {new}).
officials.whatifNote: Averages move slowly — real change needs every school improving, not just the weakest.
officials.dropIntro: Difference in average score between Grade 8 and Grade 5 (same subjects).
officials.brightIntro: Schools scoring far above their own cluster — worth a visit to learn what works. Verify the practice on the ground before showcasing.
officials.brightLine: {name} scores {score} while its cluster ({cluster}) averages {clusterScore} — {students} children assessed.
officials.heatmapIntro: Share of subject scores below the pass line (50%) in each cluster. Darker means more children struggling.
officials.concentrationLine: {pct}% of this block's struggling children are in just {schools} schools.
officials.smallN: Small groups — read scores with care.
officials.recognitionLine: On "{desc}" ({grade} {subject}), this cluster scored {observed}% — district average {district}% ({n} children).
officials.questionsTitle: Three questions for the next school meeting
officials.q1: Which children in our school are below 50%, and what extra help do they get?
officials.q2: Our cluster's weakest subject is {subject}. What is the plan for it this term?
officials.q3: A nearby school is doing much better with the same resources. Have we visited them?
officials.misconIntro: Real questions from SAKSHAM where many children chose the same wrong answer. Use them in teacher meetings — each card explains the misunderstanding.
officials.foundTitle: This year vs last year's content
officials.itemsIntro: All {n} validated items with difficulty and discrimination. Items with suspected answer-key errors are excluded.
officials.downloadsIntro: Aggregate data only — no individual student records.
officials.hubIntro: Deeper views of the SAKSHAM results — by block, cluster, item and skill.
methodsParas: SAKSHAM is a census assessment of government schools in Anugola district: 1,361 schools and 28,079 children in Grades 5 and 8, tested over two days.
Grade 5 answered 60 questions (Odia, English, Maths, EVS); Grade 8 answered 100 (Odia, English, Maths, Science, Social Studies). All questions were multiple-choice with one mark each.

A child's subject score is the percentage of that subject's questions answered correctly. A child's overall score is the average of their subject scores. A school's score is the average of its children's overall scores. The pass line used throughout is 50%.
Bands: Needs urgent support (0–25), Needs support (26–50), Developing (51–75), Excelling (76–100). Bands are derived from the displayed rounded score.

The tests are internally reliable (Cronbach's alpha 0.90 for Grade 5, 0.91 for Grade 8). A small number of questions were excluded from item-level views for test-construction issues (for example, unclear rendering or ambiguous options).
About 62% of questions tested the previous grade's content, allowing at-grade vs earlier-content comparisons. Cognitive-skill tags were assigned by hand and are reported for Grade 5 only.

School facility data comes from UDISE records. Facilities and staffing explain very little of the difference in scores between schools (about 6%) — facility data here is for entitlements and targeting, not to explain results.
This is a single round of assessment. Small schools' scores rest on few children and should be read with care. No individual child's results are published anywhere on this site.
officials.inputsIntro: Inputs shown next to the result. In Anugola these explain little of the score difference — read them for entitlement and targeting, not to explain a score.
officials.inputsNoData: No UDISE input data here yet. Request the extract for {block}.
officials.inputsBasics: Basics in place: {n} of 8 on average
officials.inputsSupport: {n} support-priority schools (low score plus a fixable input gap)
officials.blockWeakLoTitle: Weakest learning outcomes in this block
officials.outcomesIntro: Every learning outcome assessed, with the share of children answering correctly. Weakest first.
officials.dirIntro: Every assessed school with its score. Search or sort. School averages only — no individual results.
officials.neighboursIntro: The nearest schools, ordered by overall score. Where one leads this school, that is a place to look for practice to borrow.
officials.neighboursNoAhead: Not ahead of this school in any subject

v2.resExplainer: How to read your report card
v2.methodP1: SAKSHAM assesses children in Grade 5 and Grade 8 across the main subjects — Odia, English, Maths and EVS in Grade 5, and Odia, English, Maths, Science and Social Studies in Grade 8. The assessment is external, so the results are independent of the school’s own marking.
v2.methodP2: For each subject, the share of questions children answer correctly becomes the subject score. The overall score is the school’s average performance, shown out of 10 for parents (for example, 62% is 6 out of 10) and as a percentage in the Researcher view.
v2.methodP3: Every score sits in one of four bands: Excelling (above 75%), Developing (51–75%), Needs support (26–50%) and Urgent (25% and below). Only school-level averages are ever shown — never an individual child’s result.
v2.toolkitStepsT: Three questions for the next SMC meeting
v2.toolkitS1: Our school scored ___ out of 10. What is the plan to improve this year?
v2.toolkitS2: Our weakest subject is ___. What extra help will children get in it?
v2.toolkitS3: A nearby school scores higher with the same resources. What are they doing differently?
v2.toolkitHowT: How to use the report card
v2.toolkitH1: Find your school and open its report card.
v2.toolkitH2: Watch the short explainer video to understand the score.
v2.toolkitH3: Take the card to the next parent or SMC meeting and ask the three questions above.
v2.toolkitH4: Next year, check whether the score has improved.
v2.contactNoEmail: Sending will be enabled once the office email is added.

faqItems.q: What is the Saksham School Report Card?
faqItems.a: It is a simple card that shows how well children in a school are learning, based on the SAKSHAM assessment of Grade 5 and Grade 8 students in Anugola district. It gives the school one overall score out of 10, plus a score for each subject, so parents and teachers can see where the school does well and where it needs support.

faqItems.q: How is the overall score out of 10 calculated?
faqItems.a: Children answer the SAKSHAM test in each subject. The share of questions they get right becomes a percentage, and the overall score is that percentage brought to a 0–10 scale — for example, 62% shows as 6 out of 10. A higher score means more children are answering more questions correctly.

faqItems.q: What do the subject scores mean?
faqItems.a: Each subject — Odia, English, Maths, EVS, Science or Social Studies — has its own score out of 10, shown as stars. It tells you how children in that school are doing in that subject against the full mark. Low subject scores show where children need the most help.

faqItems.q: What do the colour bands mean?
faqItems.a: Every score sits in one of four bands: Excelling (above 75%), Developing (51–75%), Needs support (26–50%) and Urgent (25% and below). The band is a quick way to see where a school stands without reading the numbers.

faqItems.q: How can I use the report card as a parent?
faqItems.a: Look at your school’s score and its weakest subjects, watch the short explainer video, and take the card to the next parent or School Management Committee (SMC) meeting. Ask the school head what the plan is to improve the weak areas, and compare with nearby schools that are doing better.

SAKSHAM is a learning assessment run under the School & Mass Education Department, Government of Odisha. Grade 5 and Grade 8 children are assessed externally, so the results are independent of the school’s own marking.

faqItems.q: How often is the report card updated?
faqItems.a: The card shown here is for the 2025-26 assessment year. It is refreshed when a new round of SAKSHAM results becomes available, so you can track whether your school improves over time.

faqItems.q: Can I compare my school with other schools?
faqItems.a: Yes. Each report card lists the nearest schools with their scores, so you can see how your school compares locally. Researchers and officials can also open block-level reports that rank clusters and schools.

faqItems.q: What if my school is not listed?
faqItems.a: A few schools could not be matched to the assessment data and do not have a card yet. If you cannot find your school, please contact the District Education Office, Anugola (see Contact Us) with the school name and UDISE code.
Yes. The Researcher view offers the complete Learning-Outcome report and a data sheet (CSV), and each block report card can be downloaded as a PDF.

dataFiles.desc: One row per student per subject: school, block, cluster, grade, pseudo-ID, % correct. 128,504 rows.

dataFiles.label: Schools master (with coordinates)

dataFiles.label: Cluster league (all blocks)
dataFiles.desc: Every cluster's score, schools and students, with best and worst school.

dataFiles.label: Block × grade × subject
dataFiles.desc: Each block's average in every grade and subject.

dataFiles.label: Block aggregates
dataFiles.desc: Overall, grade-wise, % below 50 and % proficient for the eight blocks.

dataFiles.label: Learning outcomes (district, per item)
dataFiles.desc: All 151 assessed items: LO code, description, grade level, cognitive tag, % correct.

dataFiles.label: Learning outcomes by block
dataFiles.desc: Each block's strongest and weakest learning outcomes with % correct.

dataFiles.label: Misconceptions by block
dataFiles.desc: Every common-wrong-answer question per block, with options and explanation.

dataFiles.label: Item bank with difficulty & discrimination
dataFiles.desc: All validated items with psychometrics (difficulty, discrimination) for measurement research.

v2.misStimCap: Item stem / stimulus, as printed in the question paper
v2.misIndicative: * Indicative only: based on very few pupils or a single question, so read it with caution.
v2.misOrderNote: The specific wrong answer many pupils pick, with what it reveals and a teaching response. Ordered by how many pupils fell for it.
v2.privacyP1: This site shows school-level results from the SAKSHAM assessment. It never shows any individual child's result, name or personal detail.
v2.privacyP2: The site does not ask for, or store, any personal information from visitors. No login is needed. The downloadable research data is anonymised — roll numbers are removed and rows are shuffled.
v2.privacyP3: For any question about the data on this site, contact the District Education Office, Anugola (see Contact Us).
v2.termsP1: The scores and reports on this site are published by the School & Mass Education Department, Government of Odisha, from the SAKSHAM assessment of Grade 5 and Grade 8, to help parents, school heads and officials understand and improve learning.
v2.termsP2: The data may be used and shared freely for education, research and reporting, with attribution to SAKSHAM / Government of Odisha. It must not be used to rank, shame or penalise individual children or teachers.
v2.termsP3: Scores describe one assessment round and should be read together with the school's context.
v2.a11yP1: This site is built to be usable by everyone: Odia first with an English toggle, large type, high contrast, big touch targets, and it works on low-cost phones and slow connections.
v2.a11yP2: Report cards can be downloaded as PDFs, and every score is shown as text as well as stars so it does not depend on colour alone.
v2.a11yP3: If any part of this site is hard to use — with a screen reader, keyboard or otherwise — please tell us via Contact Us so we can fix it.
v2.filmDesc: What Anugola's school report card means for your child
v2.viewReportAria: View report for {name}, overall score {n} out of {max}
v2.govAboutBody: SAKSHAM is a census assessment of learning in government schools of Anugola district, Odisha. It covered 1,361 schools and 28,079 children in Grades 5 and 8, tested across six subjects over two days — so every school, block, and cluster can see where children stand and where support is needed.
v2.govAboutMoreBody: Each child answered curriculum-linked questions mapped to learning outcomes. Papers were scanned and machine-scored, and results were aggregated to school, cluster, block, and district level — individual children are never identified on this site. Scores show the percentage of questions answered correctly. Learning-outcome and misconception analyses point teachers to the specific skills that need re-teaching. Detailed methodology notes will be added here.

v2.homeAboutBody: Saksham is the Government of Odisha's initiative to assess learning outcomes of **Grade 5 and Grade 8** students in government schools. **School Report Cards** provide reliable information on learning performance to help parents, schools and education officials make **informed decisions for better learning**.
v2.dyk1D: of Grade 5 & Grade 8 students in Odisha.
v2.dyk2T: Aligned with the National Education Policy 2020
v2.dyk2D: Focus on learning outcomes and foundational skills.
v2.dyk3D: Conducted across all government schools in Anugola.
v2.dyk4D: Empowering parents, schools and officials to support every child's learning.
v2.headTipB: Search for your school or select your block to view your school's report card and key actions.
